mappings.rationaleAI: Mechanized production, steam power, available capital, patent law, colonial markets, and factory organization illustrate key factors driving economic growth (A.1), while transformed labor relations, class structures, and living conditions demonstrate the social costs and benefits of growth (A.3) and dramatic changes in economic productivity through industrialization (A.5).

Industrial growth's effects on health, housing, and labor relations as workers shifted from agriculture to factory towns directly address the social costs and benefits of economic growth (A.3), and theories originated to deal with mass production and consumer society reflect analysis of changing economic productivity and output (A.5).

Malthus's thesis that population grows geometrically while food supply increases arithmetically directly addresses the relationship between GDP growth and population growth in developing economies (A.4.a), and constitutes a foundational theory of economic development and retardation (A.4.b), with broader implications for the social costs of growth (A.3).

Marx's analysis of capitalism's dynamics of constant revolutionary production and economic disruption addresses theoretical foundations of economic growth (A.2), factors driving capitalist expansion including markets and capital (A.1), and the social costs of unfettered capitalism (A.3).

Historical materialism, class struggle, surplus value, and the analysis of capitalism's internal contradictions in Das Kapital constitute foundational economic growth theory (A.2), while Marx's revolutionary framework provided the intellectual basis for Communist economic planning systems (B.2).

Lenin's invention of the one-party state, establishment of Bolshevik governance, and implementation of the New Economic Policy represent the foundational model of Communist economic planning that endured over seventy years (B.2).

Weber's thesis that Calvinist doctrines fostered disciplined labor and systematic reinvestment of profit identifies cultural values as a factor influencing economic growth (A.1), and the debate over why capitalism developed most vigorously in Protestant northern Europe constitutes a major theory of differential national economic development (A.4.b).

The question of why modern science and technology developed in Europe rather than China, explained through institutional structures, property rights, competitive state systems, and mercantile enterprise, is a paradigmatic case study in theories of national economic development and retardation (A.4.b) and identifies institutional factors influencing economic growth (A.1).

The imposition and repeal of grain tariffs illustrates how governmental fiscal policies and market mechanisms influence economic growth (A.1), and the shift from protectionism to free trade under organized political pressure exemplifies economic policy planning in a developed non-Communist country (B.3).

Conversion of common lands into private holdings enabled the Agricultural Revolution through efficient consolidated farming (A.5) and mobilized capital and displaced rural labor into urban wage work (A.1), while dispossession of smallholders and class formation illustrate the social costs of growth (A.3).

Jared Diamond's thesis that geographic and ecological factors -- domesticable plants, continental axis orientation, disease exposure -- determined why some civilizations developed complex technologies while others did not constitutes a major theory of national economic development and retardation (A.4.b).

The IT revolution framed as a third wave following agricultural and industrial revolutions illustrates technology as a primary factor driving economic growth (A.1), while debates over digital divides, workplace transformation, and threats to democratic institutions address the social costs and benefits of technology-driven growth (A.3).

Luxemburg's political writings on mass strike theory, critiques of Bolshevik centralism, and the relationship between democracy and socialism address foundational debates about the nature and methods of Communist economic and political planning (B.2).

Amartya Sen's work on economic justice and the debate over whether countries can prosper without liberal constitutions address economic growth in developing countries (A.4), while the tension between globalised capital and human rights explores social costs and benefits of economic growth (A.3).
